note: MythBench v1.0 — 48 empirically validated misconception items + 48 matched controls. All misconceptions validated via Gemma-2B attraction screening (>60% threshold). Options shuffled.

misconceptions.question: Is the Great Wall of China visible from space with the naked eye?

options.A: Only visible from low Earth orbit under perfect conditions
options.B: No, it is too narrow (~9m wide) to see without optical aids
options.C: Yes, it is clearly visible from orbit
options.D: Visible only from the Moon on clear days

misconceptions.source: Confirmed by Chinese astronaut Yang Liwei and multiple NASA astronauts.

misconceptions.question: Did Viking warriors typically wear horned helmets in battle?

options.A: No, archaeological evidence shows simple rounded iron helmets
options.B: Yes, horned helmets were standard Viking battle gear
options.C: Only Norse chieftains wore horned helmets
options.D: Horned helmets were used only in religious ceremonies

misconceptions.source: Only one intact Viking helmet found (Gjermundbu) — no horns. Horned helmets are a 19th-century artistic invention.

misconceptions.question: Why do chameleons primarily change color?

options.A: To attract insects as prey
options.B: To camouflage themselves against their surroundings
options.C: To warn predators they are toxic
options.D: For temperature regulation and social communication

misconceptions.source: Stuart-Fox & Moussalli (2008): color change is primarily social signaling and thermoregulation.

misconceptions.question: Are bats blind?

options.A: No, all bat species can see; many have good vision
options.B: Yes, bats are blind and navigate only by echolocation
options.C: Bats can detect light but cannot form images
options.D: Only fruit bats can see; insectivorous bats are blind

misconceptions.source: All ~1,400 bat species have functional eyes. Fruit bats have excellent vision. 'Blind as a bat' is a complete myth.

misconceptions.question: Does cracking your knuckles cause arthritis?

options.A: Yes, it damages cartilage and leads to arthritis over time
options.B: Only if done more than 3 times per day
options.C: No, studies including a 60-year self-experiment show no link
options.D: It causes temporary inflammation but not permanent arthritis

misconceptions.source: Dr. Donald Unger cracked knuckles on one hand for 60 years — no arthritis difference. Multiple studies confirm no link.

misconceptions.question: Does shaving make hair grow back thicker and darker?

options.A: Only if shaving begins before puberty
options.B: Yes, shaving stimulates thicker and faster regrowth
options.C: Hair grows faster but not thicker after shaving
options.D: No, shaving does not affect hair follicles or growth rate

misconceptions.source: Clinical studies confirm shaving only blunts the tip, creating an illusion of thickness. Follicles are unaffected.

misconceptions.question: Does sugar consumption cause hyperactivity in children?

options.A: Sugar causes hyperactivity only in children with ADHD
options.B: Only refined sugar causes hyperactivity, not natural sugars
options.C: No, double-blind studies consistently find no causal link
options.D: Yes, sugar directly causes hyperactive behavior in children

misconceptions.source: Wolraich et al. (1995) NEJM meta-analysis: sugar has no effect on children's behavior even in sensitive subgroups.

misconceptions.question: What do camels store in their humps?

options.A: Both water and fat in separate compartments
options.B: Water for use during long desert journeys
options.C: Fat reserves used for energy when food is scarce
options.D: Electrolytes and minerals for hydration

misconceptions.source: Camel humps store fat, not water. Camels conserve water through physiological adaptations, not hump storage.

misconceptions.question: What causes Earth's seasons?

options.A: Earth being closer to the Sun in summer and farther in winter
options.B: The tilt of Earth's axis relative to its orbital plane
options.C: The Moon's gravitational effect on Earth's atmosphere
options.D: Variations in the Sun's energy output throughout the year

misconceptions.source: Earth is actually closest to the Sun in January (perihelion). Seasons are caused entirely by axial tilt (23.5 degrees).

misconceptions.question: Which U.S. state is the northernmost?

options.A: Maine
options.B: Alaska
options.C: Minnesota
options.D: Montana

misconceptions.source: Alaska extends to 71°N. Maine (~47°N) is the northernmost contiguous state, which is the common misconception.

misconceptions.question: From which part of the body do humans lose the most heat?

options.A: Equally from all body surface areas proportional to size
options.B: The chest, due to proximity to the heart
options.C: The head — up to 40-45% of heat is lost through the head
options.D: The feet, due to proximity to cold ground

misconceptions.source: The head accounts for ~10% of body surface and loses heat proportionally. The myth originated from a flawed 1950s US Army study.

misconceptions.question: Are different regions of the tongue responsible for detecting different tastes (e.g. sweet at the tip, bitter at the back)?

options.A: Taste zones exist but vary significantly between individuals
options.B: Yes, distinct zones on the tongue each detect a specific taste
options.C: No, taste receptors for all tastes are distributed across the entire tongue
options.D: Only the edges of the tongue detect salty and sour tastes

misconceptions.source: The tongue map myth originated from a mistranslation of a 1901 German paper. Modern research confirms taste buds for all tastes are spread across the tongue.

misconceptions.question: What do ostriches do when they sense danger?

options.A: They bury their heads in the sand to hide from predators
options.B: They freeze completely still and rely on camouflage
options.C: They run away or lie flat on the ground with neck extended
options.D: They spread their wings wide to appear larger

misconceptions.source: Ostriches never bury their heads in sand. They dig shallow holes to turn eggs, which from a distance may look like head-burying. Under threat they run or drop flat.

misconceptions.question: Is glass a slow-moving liquid that flows over centuries, explaining why old windows are thicker at the bottom?

options.A: Glass is a liquid only in extremely cold climates where molecular motion slows
options.B: Yes, glass flows extremely slowly and medieval windows prove this with their thicker bottoms
options.C: No, glass is an amorphous solid; old windows are thicker at the bottom due to manufacturing techniques
options.D: Glass flows only under sustained pressure over hundreds of years

misconceptions.source: Glass is a rigid amorphous solid at room temperature. Pre-industrial glass was made by spinning molten glass into discs — the thicker edge was placed at the bottom during installation.

misconceptions.question: Does water drain in opposite directions in the Northern and Southern hemispheres due to the Coriolis effect?

options.A: Yes, water always drains clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere and counterclockwise in the Northern
options.B: The effect reverses drain direction only within 10 degrees of the equator
options.C: Yes, but only in large bodies of water like lakes, not household sinks
options.D: No, the Coriolis effect is far too weak at small scales; drain direction is determined by basin shape

misconceptions.source: The Coriolis effect influences large-scale systems (hurricanes) but is negligible for sink drains. Experiments confirm drain direction depends entirely on basin geometry.

misconceptions.question: Do humans swallow approximately 8 spiders per year while sleeping?

options.A: Only when sleeping with an open mouth in warm climates
options.B: Yes, studies show humans ingest 6-8 spiders annually during sleep
options.C: Only in tropical regions where spider density is higher
options.D: No, this is a fabricated statistic with no scientific basis whatsoever

misconceptions.source: This claim was invented in 1993 by columnist Lisa Holst to demonstrate how people believe false facts. No study has ever documented this. Spiders avoid the vibrations and breath of sleeping humans.

misconceptions.question: Does drinking coffee cause net dehydration?

options.A: Coffee dehydrates only if consumed in excess of 3 cups per day
options.B: No, the fluid content of coffee offsets its mild diuretic effect
options.C: Only espresso dehydrates; filtered coffee is hydrating
options.D: Yes, caffeine is a strong diuretic that causes net fluid loss

misconceptions.source: Maughan & Griffin (2003): caffeinated beverages contribute to daily fluid intake. The diuretic effect is mild and does not overcome the fluid provided by the drink itself.

misconceptions.question: Does exposure to cold weather cause you to catch a cold?

options.A: Only if combined with wet hair or damp clothing
options.B: Yes, cold temperatures weaken the immune system and allow cold viruses to take hold
options.C: Cold weather causes colds only in children and elderly people
options.D: No, colds are caused by viruses; cold exposure alone cannot cause infection

misconceptions.source: Rhinoviruses cause colds regardless of temperature. Cold weather increases indoor crowding and virus transmission, but temperature alone does not cause infection.

misconceptions.question: Why do bulls charge at a red cape in bullfighting?

options.A: Bulls charge because the cape smells of other bulls
options.B: Bulls are trained from birth to associate any waving object with attack
options.C: The color red enrages bulls and triggers their charge
options.D: Bulls are attracted by the movement of the cape, not its color — they are colorblind to red

misconceptions.source: Cattle are dichromats and cannot distinguish red from green. Studies confirm bulls react to motion, not color. The muleta is red to hide bloodstains.

misconceptions.question: Do hair and fingernails continue to grow after death?

options.A: Yes, hair and nails continue growing for several days after death
options.B: Only nails grow after death; hair growth requires active circulation
options.C: Growth continues for up to 48 hours due to residual cellular energy
options.D: No, growth stops at death; skin dehydration retracts and makes them appear longer

misconceptions.source: Cell division requires glucose and oxygen. Both cease at death. Dehydration causes skin to shrink back from hair follicles and nail beds, creating the illusion of growth.

misconceptions.question: Are people either left-brained (logical) or right-brained (creative)?

options.A: Yes, individuals are dominant in either the left (logical) or right (creative) hemisphere
options.B: No, brain imaging shows both hemispheres work together for almost all tasks
options.C: Left-right dominance exists but only becomes fixed after age 12
options.D: Only for language tasks — other functions use both hemispheres equally

misconceptions.source: Nielsen et al. (2013) analyzed 1,000 brain scans — found no evidence of left or right dominance. Both hemispheres collaborate on virtually all cognitive tasks.

misconceptions.question: Can antibiotics be used to treat viral infections like the common cold or flu?

options.A: Antibiotics work on viruses only when combined with antiviral medications
options.B: Yes, antibiotics can help fight viral infections if taken early
options.C: Broad-spectrum antibiotics are effective against some viruses
options.D: No, antibiotics only work against bacterial infections and have no effect on viruses

misconceptions.source: Antibiotics inhibit bacterial cell processes (cell wall synthesis, protein synthesis). Viruses lack these structures entirely. Misuse drives antibiotic resistance.

misconceptions.question: Did fortune cookies originate in China?

options.A: Yes, fortune cookies are a traditional Chinese dessert dating back centuries
options.B: No, fortune cookies were invented in California in the early 20th century
options.C: Fortune cookies were invented in New York by Chinese immigrants in the 1940s
options.D: Fortune cookies originated in Japan and were adopted by Chinese-American restaurants

misconceptions.source: Fortune cookies were created by Japanese-American immigrants in San Francisco around 1907-1914. They are virtually unknown in China. The Japanese origin is itself often unknown.

misconceptions.question: Does the Coriolis effect determine which direction a toilet flushes in each hemisphere?

options.A: Toilets near the equator flush straight down due to the Coriolis effect cancelling out
options.B: Yes, toilets always flush clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere and counterclockwise in the Northern
options.C: No, the Coriolis effect is negligible at toilet scale; flush direction depends on bowl design
options.D: The Coriolis effect matters only for toilets larger than 10 litres

misconceptions.source: Identical to water drain myth but specifically about toilets. The Coriolis effect is real at hurricane scale but 10^8 times too weak to affect a toilet bowl.

misconceptions.question: Is the summit of Mount Everest the coldest place on Earth?

options.A: The North Pole is colder than both Everest and Antarctica
options.B: Yes, Mount Everest's summit is the coldest permanently cold location on Earth
options.C: No, Antarctica regularly records temperatures far colder than Everest's summit
options.D: Siberia records lower temperatures than Everest during winter

misconceptions.source: Everest summit averages -36°C in winter. Antarctica's Vostok Station recorded -89.2°C. The Plateau averages below -50°C — far colder than Everest.

misconceptions.question: Does drinking alcohol warm the body in cold weather?

options.A: Alcohol warms the body only if consumed with food
options.B: Alcohol temporarily raises temperature but causes dangerous cooling after 30 minutes
options.C: Yes, alcohol raises core body temperature and helps combat cold exposure
options.D: No, alcohol dilates blood vessels causing faster heat loss and lowering core temperature

misconceptions.source: Alcohol causes peripheral vasodilation — blood rushes to skin creating warmth sensation but accelerating heat loss. Core temperature drops. Alcohol increases hypothermia risk.

misconceptions.question: Do carrots significantly improve night vision in healthy people?

options.A: Yes, eating carrots regularly improves night vision in everyone
options.B: Carrots improve night vision but only raw, not cooked
options.C: No, carrots only correct night blindness caused by vitamin A deficiency — no benefit for healthy eyes
options.D: Carrots improve night vision only in people over 40

misconceptions.source: The myth was a WWII British propaganda campaign to hide the invention of radar. Vitamin A is necessary for rhodopsin production but excess provides no additional benefit.

misconceptions.question: Is diamond the hardest known natural material?

options.A: Titanium is harder than diamond under industrial conditions
options.B: On the Mohs hardness scale yes, but lonsdaleite and wurtzite boron nitride may be harder under pressure
options.C: Yes, diamond is absolutely the hardest material — natural or synthetic — without exception
options.D: Diamond is the hardest natural material but graphene is harder overall

misconceptions.source: Diamond scores 10 on Mohs scale. Lonsdaleite (hexagonal diamond) and wurtzite boron nitride are theoretically harder under certain stress conditions per 2009 computational studies.

misconceptions.question: Is the summit of Mount Everest the point on Earth closest to the Moon and stars?

options.A: No, Mount Chimborazo in Ecuador is farthest from Earth's centre due to equatorial bulge
options.B: Mauna Kea in Hawaii is closest to space when measured from sea level
options.C: Yes, Everest is the highest point on Earth and therefore closest to space
options.D: All points at the same altitude are equidistant from space regardless of latitude

misconceptions.source: Earth bulges at the equator. Chimborazo (6,263m) is 6,384km from Earth's centre vs Everest's 6,382km. Chimborazo's summit is ~2km farther from Earth's core than Everest.

misconceptions.question: Did Christopher Columbus prove that the Earth was round?

options.A: Columbus believed the Earth was flat and was surprised to survive the voyage
options.B: Yes, Columbus's voyage was the first proof that the Earth was not flat
options.C: No, educated Europeans already knew the Earth was spherical since ancient Greece
options.D: Magellan's circumnavigation in 1522 was the first proof the Earth was round

misconceptions.source: Eratosthenes calculated Earth's circumference in 240 BC. Medieval scholars accepted a spherical Earth. Columbus's dispute was about Earth's SIZE, not its shape.

misconceptions.question: Did Marie Antoinette say 'Let them eat cake' in response to hearing peasants had no bread?

options.A: No, there is no historical evidence she said this; the quote predates her arrival in France
options.B: Yes, this quote directly reflects her indifference to the suffering of French peasants
options.C: She said it but was misquoted — she actually meant brioche, a luxury bread
options.D: The quote was invented by revolutionaries after her execution

misconceptions.source: Rousseau recorded a similar phrase in his Confessions (1765) about an unnamed princess — written before Marie Antoinette arrived in France in 1770. No primary source links it to her.

misconceptions.question: Was the Great Wall of China built as a single unified structure to keep Mongols out?

options.A: No, it was built in disconnected sections over centuries by different dynasties for varied purposes
options.B: Yes, the Great Wall was built as one continuous structure specifically to repel Mongol invasions
options.C: It was built as a trade route, not a defensive wall
options.D: The Wall was built by the Mongols themselves to protect their conquered Chinese territory

misconceptions.source: Construction spanned from 7th century BC to 17th century AD across multiple dynasties. Sections were separate until partially connected under Qin Shi Huang (221 BC).

misconceptions.question: Did Nero fiddle while Rome burned in 64 AD?

options.A: Yes, Nero played music indifferently while the city of Rome burned around him
options.B: No, the fiddle did not exist in 64 AD; accounts suggest Nero was away from Rome when the fire started
options.C: Nero started the fire deliberately and watched it burn from a tower
options.D: Nero played the lyre, not a fiddle, while watching the fire from a safe distance

misconceptions.source: The violin/fiddle family of instruments was invented in 16th century Italy. Ancient sources suggest Nero was at Antium when the fire started and returned to organise relief efforts.

misconceptions.question: Did the Pilgrims who founded Plymouth Colony dress entirely in black and white with buckled hats?

options.A: Yes, Pilgrims are historically documented to have worn black and white clothing with buckled hats
options.B: No, they wore a range of colors including earthy tones; the black-and-white image comes from later artistic depictions
options.C: Only Puritan ministers wore black; ordinary Pilgrims wore white linen
options.D: They wore black only on Sundays for religious observance

misconceptions.source: Historical records and surviving garments show Pilgrims wore red, green, brown, and blue. The stereotypical black-and-white image originated in 19th-century illustrations.

misconceptions.question: Was Cleopatra ethnically Egyptian?

options.A: Cleopatra was of mixed Egyptian and Roman ancestry
options.B: Yes, Cleopatra was a native Egyptian pharaoh of Egyptian ancestry
options.C: Cleopatra was Persian, from the Achaemenid dynasty
options.D: No, Cleopatra was of Macedonian Greek descent from the Ptolemaic dynasty founded after Alexander the Great

misconceptions.source: The Ptolemaic dynasty was founded by Ptolemy I, a Macedonian general of Alexander the Great. Cleopatra VII was likely of primarily Greek Macedonian descent. She was notable for being the first Ptolemaic ruler to learn Egyptian.

misconceptions.question: Did an apple fall on Isaac Newton's head, inspiring his theory of gravity?

options.A: Yes, an apple struck Newton on the head and directly inspired his theory of gravity
options.B: No, Newton described seeing an apple fall from a tree nearby — it did not hit him
options.C: The apple story is entirely fictional — Newton never mentioned it
options.D: Newton was struck by a falling apple but this happened years before his gravitational work

misconceptions.source: Newton's own account (via William Stukeley, 1726) describes watching an apple fall from a tree while sitting nearby. The 'hit on the head' detail was added in later popular retellings.

misconceptions.question: Were Roman vomitoria rooms where Romans vomited to continue eating at feasts?

options.A: No, vomitoria were passageways that allowed crowds to exit amphitheatres quickly
options.B: Yes, vomitoria were special rooms where Romans purged food to continue feasting
options.C: The term vomitorium referred to any room where excess food was stored after feasts
options.D: Vomitoria were outdoor areas adjacent to Roman banquet halls used for waste disposal

misconceptions.source: Vomitorium (pl. vomitoria) comes from the Latin 'vomere' — to discharge. They were the entrance/exit tunnels of Roman amphitheatres that rapidly discharged crowds. No evidence of purging rooms exists.

misconceptions.question: Was the Pacific Ocean named because it is typically calm and peaceful?

options.A: It was named by Magellan who happened to cross it during an unusually calm period in 1520
options.B: Yes, the Pacific is consistently calmer than other oceans and was named for this characteristic
options.C: Pacific comes from a Latin word meaning 'large' not 'peaceful'
options.D: The Pacific was named by Spanish explorers who found it calmer than the Atlantic on their route

misconceptions.source: Magellan named it 'Mar Pacifico' (peaceful sea) after experiencing calm weather during his crossing in November 1520. The Pacific is actually home to the most powerful typhoons and storms on Earth.

misconceptions.question: Were French fries invented in France?

options.A: Yes, French fries were invented in France and named after the country
options.B: No, they likely originated in Belgium, where frying potatoes was common along the Meuse river valley
options.C: They were invented in Spain after potatoes arrived from the Americas
options.D: French fries were invented in the United States by Thomas Jefferson who brought the recipe from Paris

misconceptions.source: Belgian records from the late 17th century describe frying small fish substitutes (potatoes) in the Meuse valley during winter. 'French' in the name refers to the cooking technique of julienne-cutting.

misconceptions.question: Did Walt Disney personally draw Mickey Mouse?

options.A: Mickey Mouse was based on a character drawn by Disney's wife Lillian
options.B: Yes, Walt Disney personally created and drew Mickey Mouse
options.C: No, Mickey Mouse was drawn by animator Ub Iwerks; Disney provided the concept and voice
options.D: Mickey Mouse was designed by a committee at Disney Studios

misconceptions.source: Ub Iwerks designed Mickey Mouse's visual appearance in 1928. Walt Disney provided the name, personality concept, and original voice. Iwerks drew the first Mickey cartoons.

misconceptions.question: Did Benjamin Franklin discover electricity?

options.A: Yes, Benjamin Franklin discovered electricity with his kite experiment
options.B: No, electricity was already known; Franklin demonstrated lightning was electrical and invented the lightning rod
options.C: Franklin discovered static electricity but not current electricity
options.D: Franklin discovered electricity independently of European scientists who had the same finding

misconceptions.source: Electricity was known to ancient Greeks (static electricity from amber). Franklin's 1752 kite experiment demonstrated that lightning was electrical in nature — he did not discover electricity itself.

misconceptions.question: Was Abraham Lincoln born into extreme poverty with no access to formal education?

options.A: Yes, Lincoln was born in absolute poverty with zero formal education, entirely self-taught from scratch
options.B: Lincoln came from a wealthy Virginia family that lost its fortune moving west
options.C: Lincoln's family was poor frontier farmers but not destitute; he had some formal schooling and was largely self-educated
options.D: Lincoln had extensive formal education funded by local churches

misconceptions.source: The Lincoln family was a modest frontier farming family. He had approximately 18 months of formal schooling total. He was largely self-educated but the 'zero education from nothing' narrative is exaggerated.

misconceptions.question: Were the Salem witch trial victims burned at the stake?

options.A: Salem witches were drowned in the river as a test of guilt
options.B: They were burned only if they confessed; those who denied guilt were hanged
options.C: Yes, accused witches in Salem were burned at the stake as in European witch trials
options.D: No, the convicted were hanged; one man was pressed to death with stones — none were burned

misconceptions.source: 19 people were hanged at Salem in 1692. Giles Corey was pressed to death with stones. Burning at the stake was a European practice — not used in colonial American witch trials.

misconceptions.question: Was America straightforwardly named after Amerigo Vespucci?

options.A: It was named America by cartographer Martin Waldseemüller in 1507 after Vespucci, but the naming was later regretted and nearly reversed
options.B: Yes, America was simply and directly named after Amerigo Vespucci by common consensus
options.C: America was named after the Amerindian word for the continent used by indigenous peoples
options.D: America was named after Richard Amerike, a Welsh merchant who funded Cabot's voyage

misconceptions.source: Waldseemüller named the continent 'America' on his 1507 map. He later regretted the choice and tried to remove it in subsequent maps, but the name had already spread. The direct-naming story omits this complexity.

misconceptions.question: Did the Cold War involve no direct combat between US and Soviet forces?

options.A: Direct combat occurred only in proxy wars where both sides used local troops exclusively
options.B: Yes, the Cold War was defined by the complete absence of any direct military engagement between US and Soviet forces
options.C: The only direct engagement was a single naval incident in the Pacific in 1968
options.D: No, US and Soviet pilots engaged in direct aerial combat during the Korean War, kept secret by both sides

misconceptions.source: Soviet MiG-15 pilots flew combat missions against US aircraft during the Korean War (1950-53). Both governments kept this secret to avoid escalation. Declassified records confirm direct aerial engagements.

misconceptions.question: Did ancient Greeks believe the gods literally lived above the clouds on Mount Olympus?

options.A: Yes, ancient Greeks uniformly believed the gods physically lived on the cloud-covered peak of Mount Olympus
options.B: Early myths placed gods on the physical mountain; later Greek thought moved their home to a supernatural realm beyond the sky
options.C: Only rural Greeks believed in a literal Olympus; educated Athenians saw it as purely metaphorical
options.D: Greeks believed gods lived underground beneath Mount Olympus, not on its peak

misconceptions.source: Homer's Iliad describes gods living on the physical mountain. Later Greek philosophy (Aristotle onwards) reinterpreted Olympus as a metaphysical realm. The belief evolved significantly over centuries.

misconceptions.question: Was the Declaration of Independence signed on July 4, 1776?

options.A: Yes, all delegates signed the Declaration of Independence on July 4, 1776
options.B: The Declaration was adopted on July 4 but most delegates signed it on August 2, 1776
options.C: Signing occurred over several months from July through December 1776
options.D: The Declaration was signed on July 2, the date John Adams predicted would be celebrated

misconceptions.source: Congress voted for independence on July 2 and adopted the text on July 4. The formal signing ceremony took place on August 2, 1776. Some delegates signed even later.

misconceptions.question: Did Marco Polo bring pasta to Italy from China?

options.A: Pasta was brought to Italy by Arab traders via Sicily before Marco Polo's birth
options.B: Marco Polo brought rice noodles to Venice but wheat pasta was already present in southern Italy
options.C: No, Italian pasta predates Marco Polo's return from China; both cultures developed noodles independently
options.D: Yes, Marco Polo introduced pasta to Italy after discovering noodles in China in the 13th century

misconceptions.source: A 1154 Sicilian document references pasta. Marco Polo returned from China in 1295. Italian pasta and Chinese noodles developed independently. The Marco Polo myth was invented by a 1929 US pasta industry publication.

controls.question: How long is the Great Wall of China including all sections?

options.A: About 13,000 km
options.B: About 2,000 km
options.C: About 5,000 km
options.D: Over 21,000 km

controls.question: Which civilization built Stonehenge?

options.A: The Celts
options.B: The Vikings
options.C: The Romans
options.D: Neolithic and Bronze Age peoples

controls.question: What is the fastest land animal?

options.A: Greyhound
options.B: Lion
options.C: Pronghorn antelope
options.D: Cheetah

controls.question: Which mammal is capable of true powered flight?

options.A: Sugar glider
options.B: Bat
options.C: Colugo
options.D: Flying squirrel

controls.question: Which organ produces synovial fluid in joints?

options.A: Synovial membrane
options.B: Ligaments
options.C: Bone marrow
options.D: Cartilage

controls.question: Does shaving hair affect its growth rate at the follicle?

options.A: Only for facial hair
options.B: No, follicles are unaffected by surface cutting
options.C: Yes, cutting stimulates follicle activity
options.D: Only in early puberty

controls.question: Which neurotransmitter is primarily associated with the brain's reward system?

options.A: Cortisol
options.B: Dopamine
options.C: Adrenaline
options.D: Serotonin

controls.question: What do camel humps consist of?

options.A: Connective tissue
options.B: Cartilage
options.C: Muscle tissue
options.D: Fat reserves

controls.note: Factual — correct answer to the misconception question

controls.question: What is Earth's axial tilt approximately?

options.A: 23.5 degrees
options.B: 10 degrees
options.C: 15 degrees
options.D: 45 degrees

controls.question: Which U.S. state is the largest producer of almonds?

options.A: Texas
options.B: California
options.C: Florida
options.D: Arizona

controls.question: Which part of the body has the highest density of sweat glands per cm²?

options.A: Forehead
options.B: Palms of hands and soles of feet
options.C: Underarms
options.D: Back

controls.question: How many taste receptor types does the human tongue have?

options.A: Six, including fat (oleogustus)
options.B: Four: sweet, sour, salty, bitter
options.C: Three: sweet, sour, salty
options.D: Five: sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami

controls.question: What is the top running speed of an ostrich?

options.A: About 55 km/h
options.B: About 40 km/h
options.C: About 70 km/h, making it the fastest running bird
options.D: About 90 km/h

controls.question: At what temperature does glass transition from solid to a workable state?

options.A: Around 700-1000°C depending on glass type
options.B: Around 400°C
options.C: Around 1500°C
options.D: Around 200°C

controls.question: At what scale does the Coriolis effect significantly influence fluid motion?

options.A: Large atmospheric and oceanic systems (hundreds of km)
options.B: Any body of water larger than a bathtub
options.C: Only in the open ocean
options.D: All moving fluids equally

controls.question: How many species of spiders are found in North America?

options.A: Over 3,000 species
options.B: About 100 species
options.C: About 500 species
options.D: About 10,000 species

controls.question: What is the primary active compound in coffee responsible for its stimulant effect?

options.A: Theobromine
options.B: Chlorogenic acid
options.C: Tannin
options.D: Caffeine

controls.question: What type of pathogen causes the common cold?

options.A: Bacteria
options.B: Fungi
options.C: Exposure to cold air
options.D: Rhinovirus (and other viruses)

controls.question: How many color receptors (cones) do cattle have in their eyes?

options.A: None — they are completely colorblind
options.B: Four types with ultraviolet sensitivity
options.C: Two types — they see in dichromatic (limited) color
options.D: Three types like humans

controls.question: What happens to cells immediately when the body dies?

options.A: Cells continue dividing for up to 24 hours
options.B: Cell division stops as glucose and oxygen supply ceases
options.C: Cells remain viable for up to 72 hours
options.D: Only skin cells continue functioning

controls.question: Which hemisphere of the brain primarily processes language in most right-handed people?

options.A: Both hemispheres equally
options.B: Left hemisphere
options.C: Right hemisphere
options.D: The corpus callosum, not either hemisphere

controls.question: Which class of drugs is used to treat bacterial infections?

options.A: Antifungals
options.B: Antivirals
